Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. Рђд. With water: Beethoven (conducted by Frank Zappa). Finish: as long as a Fidel speech. Amazing combination of all things from the sea with all smokes (yeah, including that one) and all citrus fruits. Even salted kumquats. Comments: my only regret is that this is no surprise. These late 1960s Bowmores for various Italian condottieri have all been utterly stunning. Mamma mia! SGP:467 - 96 points.

whisky_name_raw: King's Legend (40%, Ainslie's, blend, +/-1975)
review_text: King's Legend (40%, Ainslie's, blend, +/-1975) KingРђЎs Legend was an old brand, IРђЎve also got much older bottlings but I thought weРђЎd rather go for an Рђўaperitive-yРђЎ version today. You'll find notes for an older version on this website (WF 88). Colour: gold. Nose: this baby reeks of old Clynelish. ItРђЎs extremely mineral and waxy, with good OBE, ashes, touches of iron (old tin box), quite some paraffin, motor oil, a little camphor and just touches of English brown sauce, possibly from OBE. High malt content, IРђЎm sure. Mouth: itРђЎs a punchy blend, quite peaty, ashy and smoky. Above all, itРђЎs very mineral and paraffiny while thereРђЎs also quite some pepper as a base. ItРђЎs got a Talisker side. Finish: loses points now because of the extreme dryness and the cardboardy side thatРђЎs Рђўtoo muchРђЎ. Comments: a dry and austere blend with high malt content and definitely an Рђўold HighlandsРђЎ style. Deliciously un-modern. The only fruitiness youРђЎll find is a little marmalade, or orange zests. SGP:254 - 85 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glen Brora (40%, Carradale Blending Co, blend, +/-1970)
review_text: Glen Brora (40%, Carradale Blending Co, blend, +/-1970) Carradale was one of Ainslie & HeilbronnРђЎs sister companies. Theyve also bottled King's Legend at times. IРђЎve already downed quite a few bottles of this little baby but never thought I had to come up with proper tasting notes. NowРђЎs the time. By the way, beware of some dodgy people whoРђЎd try to sell you this for a very old Brora. Colour: full gold. Nose: a fuller, richer, maltier version of the KingРђЎs Legend. ThereРђЎs probably more caramel inside РђЊ which is what the much darker colour suggested anyway РђЊ but thereРђЎs also more sherry wood, more chicken soup, more tobacco, a bigger sootiness and probably more smoke. What could be is that the Old Clynelish thatРђЎs inside was direct-fired whilst the KingРђЎs LegendРђЎs Clynelish was already steam-heated. I mean the two stills ;-). The changes occurred in 1961 at Clynelish. WhatРђЎs sure is that the Glen Brora is a bigger, fatter spirit on the nose. Mouth: itРђЎs huge! Imagine, only 40% vol and already a good forty years in glass. It did not lose 1%, but itРђЎs true the level was high. Once again, we have more or less the style of the KingРђЎs Liqueur but with more body, greasiness, fatness, caramel, fudge, cigars, spices, pepper, soot, ashes, liquorice, saltРђд I donРђЎt think many modern malts could hand a candle to this old baby. And I love the menthol as wellРђд Finish: itРђЎs where the difference with the KL is huge. This oneРђЎs long, peppery, salty, thereРђЎs some fudge, bitter oranges, gingerbread, juniper, chlorophyllРђд Wow! Comments: so yeah, itРђЎs no Brora despite the name, but itРђЎs as good as any B. Almost. Now, thereРђЎs probably 50% Old Clynelish inside, if not more. SGP:454 - 90 points.

whisky_name_raw: Clynelish 12 yo (70┬░ proof, OB, spring cap, no neck label, Ainslie's capsule, +/-1960?)
review_text: Clynelish 12 yo (70┬░ proof, OB, spring cap, no neck label, Ainslie's capsule, +/-1960?) The only places where I could find these ultra-rare spring cap versions (I know three different versions altogether) were cases of Рђўtwist-capРђЎ Clynelishes bottled in the late 1960s. ItРђЎs a funny story, it seems that the distillers used to try to get rid of these old unsellable bottles that way. No harm, no fool! So itРђЎs a nice lottery, every time you open a new (well) case of Old Clynelish 12 cream label, you might find one bottle bearing a spring cap. Did Willy Wonka use to be the manager at Clynelish? Colour: gold. Nose: good, this is immense. ItРђЎs not immense because of the alcohol, that part is relatively low, itРђЎs immense because of all the shoe polish, the waxes, the soot, the pitch, the almonds and then the various oils. ThatРђЎs whatРђЎs striking, the notes of oils such as graphite, linseed, oliveРђд ItРђЎs almost like putting your nose into the exhausts of an old Aston Martin (haha). ThereРђЎs even a touch of vegetables. Maybe beans? Also hay in a warm summer day. LetРђЎs move on if you pleaseРђд Mouth: the most intense whisky at 40% vol. I could ever try. This baby was a little closed when we opened it with some good friends up there on location, but now itРђЎs doing Рђўthe peacockРђЎs tailРђЎ and the flavours are totally stunning (if you like waxy dry whiskies, that is, if youРђЎre rather into bananas or maple syrup youРђЎll probably hate this). Where was I? So yeah, oils again, coal, tar liqueur (we shall try the rare Clacquessin soon on WF!), oysters, cigar ashes, teas of all sorts, these touches of vegetables again (IРђЎd say artichokes this time), old cough syrup (the eucalyptus side never stops growing, like in many very old whiskies in my experience). Oh well, better call the anti-maltoporn brigade! Finish: all right, all right, itРђЎs maybe not extremely long, but the cleanliness is amazing. ItРђЎs akin to the finish of the fifth waters of an ultra-old pre-Mao Pu-erh tea from the seventh sacred mountain. Right, I just made that up. Comments: unnecessary, I think. The highest you could go at 40% vol. Oh, forgot to say, itРђЎs also very peaty whisky. SGP:365 - 94 points.

whisky_name_raw: Brora 13 yo 1982/1996 (59.9%, Cadenhead, Authentic Collection)
review_text: Brora 13 yo 1982/1996 (59.9%, Cadenhead, Authentic Collection) Not even 60% vol., pfff.. ;-) IРђЎve already tried two punchy young 1982s by W.M. Cadenhead but IРђЎm afraid I never took notes. They used to score around WF 88, in case that matters. Oh come onРђд Colour: straw. Nose: ouch! Raw alcohol, aggressive, pungent and very un-aromatic. Ultra-grassy. So yeah, raw power as Iggy would say, water is much needed. With water: it became very bubblegumy. We all know some late batches at Brora have been totally unpeated and not even waxy/sooty. Well, this is from one of them. Smells like a young Glenmorangie РђЊ honest! Mouth (neat): itРђЎs absolutely fabulous to be able to try a distillate such as Brora when it was still young and, above all, from a РђўlightРђЎ cask. In fact, this is much fruitier than expected again, I even get a lot of strawberries and pears, and thereРђЎs even a feeling of marshmallows and grapefruit-flavoured Jell-O. Does that exist? Really! With water: same, an easy, fruity, young malt. Fruit liqueurs and violet bonbons. A little aniseed as well. Finish: rather long and just as fruity. ThereРђЎs even a little pastis. CРђЎest vrai ! Nice pink grapefruit in the aftertaste. Comments: the most un-Brora Brora ever, but it remains very good whisky, mind youРђд Those two stills were (are!) fabulous. SGP:631 - 88 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glenfarclas 1953/2013 'Auld Alliance' (43.9%, OB, Spanish sherry butt, cask #1682, 125 bottles)
review_text: Glenfarclas 1953/2013 'Auld Alliance' (43.9%, OB, Spanish sherry butt, cask #1682, 125 bottles) This 59 years old baby is part of a Рѓг15,000 duo and comes with a Hine Cognac from the same vintage, hence the name ' Auld Alliance '. Nope I haven't got the cognac! Colour: full gold. Nose: it's very close to the 1953 cask #1674 for Wealth Solutions, only a notch oakier and a little more aromatic as well, which makes them even. Sort of. Quite a lot of leather and tobacco, then overripe tropical fruits (mangos) and some mentholated oak extracts. The bacon's also louder than in the sister cask and then some whiffs of damp earth do arise. What's impressive is the freshness and the fact that in no way it's simply a sherry bomb. Mouth: almost identical (I'm comparing it with cask #1674 as we speak). A lot of oak spices, tangerines, a little vin jaune (right-o, dry sherry), smoked ham and Virginia tobacco. It feels old and impressively fresh at the same time, which is quite a miracle although, as almost always at this age, the nose was more entrancing and, say vibrant. Finish: pretty long and with a grassiness from the old oak. Comments: the opposite of a dead old whisky. You would think the oak would have taken over (but hey, I doubt they would have bottled it in that case) but it didn't. An ode to old age! SGP:572 - 92 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glenisla 34 yo 1977/2011 (44.3%, Signatory Vintage, cask #19605, 274 bottles)
review_text: Glenisla 34 yo 1977/2011 (44.3%, Signatory Vintage, cask #19605, 274 bottles) Glenisla was made using peaty water from the Outer Hebrides, thatРђЎs been concentrated via distillation. Iain Henderson, who was yet to become LaphroaigРђЎs manager (obviously), had conducted the experiments for Chivas. IРђЎve only tried Glenisla once so far, and found it a little unlikely (WF 75). Oh and the one weРђЎre having today was distilled on 7/7/77 and bottled on 11/11/11. How smart is that? Colour: white wine. Nose: oh what a strange nose! Heavy liquorice and coffee at first nosing, with plenty of sour wood as well, dustРђд It does smell a bit like some kind of coffee-flavoured fudge, in fact. ItРђЎs also a little yoghurty around the edges, before a very odd kind of smokiness arises, hard to describe. I think I wonРђЎt even tryРђд Some fermenting hay too. Mouth: very strange indeed. A bizarre grassiness, quite sour, notes of cheese, some kind of stale beer, some lemon juiceРђд And then more pepper. Also this strange feeling of stale peaty water, but that may well be my mindРђЎs work. I donРђЎt seem to remember IРђЎve tried too much peaty water anyway, even during the wildest Islay days. Finish: medium length, a little better. Tinned pineapples? ItРђЎs more citrusy too, and thatРђЎs pleasant. Comments: this one is very hard to score. ItРђЎs probably more of historical interest than organoleptically appealing, even though itРђЎs got some pleasant sides. A great bottling, but maybe not for the best reasons. SGP:442 - 72 points.

whisky_name_raw: Craigduff 40 yo 1973/2013 (49.6%, Signatory Vintage, cask #2516, 616 bottles)
review_text: Craigduff 40 yo 1973/2013 (49.6%, Signatory Vintage, cask #2516, 616 bottles) This baby was bottled by Signatory exactly 40 years after it was distilled, on April 4th, 1973. So 4/4/73 to 4/4/13. It seems that itРђЎs also been made at Glen Keith, so earlier than the Glenisla. Not too sureРђд The Whisky Exchange have got a nice explanation by Andrew Symington (owner of Signatory) but whatРђЎs still a little unclear is what are the technical differences between Glenisla and Craigduff. Colour: gold. Nose: not that different from the Glenisla for a wee while, but it gets then much fruitier and, letРђЎs say it, pleasant. We have mainly oranges and orange squash, funny hints of tamarind, a bubblegumy side that we often find in ex-Lomond stills malt whisky (Mosstowie and such)Рђд Also touches of overripe bananas and maybe even a little sugar cane. A very good surprise so far. After ten minutes: more vanilla. Mouth: once again, this is much, much better. ItРђЎs got something of old Ladyburns or Inverlevens if I remember well, with a rather massive combination of oaky vanilla with oranges and lemons. I do not get any peat, I have to say, but itРђЎs a fine spirit, no doubt. Not just Рђўfor the recordРђЎ. Nice maple syrup too. Finish: long and much more citrusy. A very zesty finish, very lemony. Cool! Comments: this Craigduff defeats the Glenisla hands down. I also think itРђЎs funny that while trying to come up with a peaty Islay-style whisky, they ended up making some Lowlander ;-). Although thereРђЎs more peatiness in the aftertaste. Worth trying, really. SGP:651 - 86 points.

whisky_name_raw: Ardbeg 'Batch 4' (52.4%, That Boutique-y Whisky Company, 427 bottles)
review_text: Ardbeg 'Batch 4' (52.4%, That Boutique-y Whisky Company, 427 bottles) Batch 1 has been excellent! Not too sure how they РђўbakeРђЎ these elusive batches I have to say, but itРђЎs always fun to see a good friend on a label. Colour: gold. Nose: itРђЎs typical modern Ardbeg, with probably less tarry and straightly phenolic notes than older ones, but maybe more brine and seawater on the one side, and garden bonfire on the other side (burning leaves and hay). ThereРђЎs a feeling of menthol cigarettes РђЊ their smoke РђЊ and then a pretty massive development on all things medicinal, especially antiseptic. Also a newly opened pack of smoked almonds? Even heavily smoked salmon? Nice complexity. After fifteen minutes: more lemon juice. With water: yess! It became both drier and (even) smokier. Motor oil, hot tarmac in the middle of July ;-), this menthol again, a plate of smoked fishРђд And grapefruits. Mouth (neat): starts as sweet as Ardbeg can be, before the heavy smoke kicks in. So vanilla and crystallised lemon, then an acrid smokiness thatРђЎs exactly what we were expecting. This feeling of Рђўeating an ashtrayРђЎ. This oneРђЎs also moderately salty this time. With water: excellent. I donРђЎt know if this is a vatting from various sources but it does taste like a single cask, meaning itРђЎs very РђўfocusedРђЎ now, very РђўpreciseРђЎ. Beautiful lemon, almond oil and touches of seashells. Say clams. Perfect smoke. Finish: long, not that salty, smoky, ashy and Ardbeggy. You bet! Comments: IРђЎm very happy. I think this is a style that the owners (of the distillery) should produce too. SGP:468 - 90 points.

whisky_name_raw: Ardbeg 19 yo 1993/2013 (57.9%, Cadenhead, bourbon hoghsead, 302 bottles)
review_text: Ardbeg 19 yo 1993/2013 (57.9%, Cadenhead, bourbon hoghsead, 302 bottles) This charming little baby was bottled right this month. I believe itРђЎs from these very small batches that the Laphroaig crew used to make from time to time while Ardbeg was almost dormant. Colour: white wine. Nose: much more austere than the NAS, grassier, smokier, with even some mustard and a lot of earth and bitter roots. In other words, itРђЎs very gentiany on the nose. Just like the previous one, itРђЎs also got a lot of grass smoke and then more tar and pitch, which was less there in the NAS. Also seaweed and maybe pickled samphires. Ever tried that? After ten minutes: these big notes of a working kiln, very РђўArdbegРђЎ. After fifteen minutes: a little more sweeter aromas, but not much. Grapefruits? With water: wild! Burnt butter, coal, hessian (old forgotten bags) and a lot of ashes and smoke. The peatines is quite massive. Mouth (neat): yes! This sharp, chiselled, slightly oily (and fruity) arrival is unmistakenly Ardbeg. Big ashes, big lemon drops (I mean a lot of them), a lot of gentian and this astringent and acrid profile that keeps growing and growing. Olive oil, a lot of that. Maybe a wee hint of cardboard too, but thatРђЎs nothing. Powerful ultra-clean Ardbeg. With water: itРђЎs a wee bit less focused than the NAS at this point, there are drops of seawater that we love in the nose but maybe not always on the palate. The rest is perfect, very kippery, with perfect citrus. Straight lime juice. Finish: long, a little grassy and generally green. Rhubarb? (thatРђЎs certainly green!) The aftertaste is saltier than the BoutiqueРђЎs. Comments: very high quality again. Maybe this one is a wee notch less immediately РђўwowРђЎ and has a few very tiny shaky aspects, but other than that, itРђЎs a superb Ardbeg thatРђЎll beat many more well-known ones. Hands down! SGP:467 - 89 points.

Tamdhu 10 yo (40%, OB, +/-1995) Colour: gold. Nose: itРђЎs a lovely mentholated and malty nose, I even get herbs such as rosemary and thyme. Quite some sage too, which is rare. Then we have more cider apples, apple skins, fresh almonds and walnuts and a wee plankish side thatРђЎs far from being unpleasant. Truly a lovely mentholated nose, much nicer than I remembered. After ten minutes: huge notes of peaches, wow! Mouth: peaches again, but also more indistinct flavours, some tea and bergamot (earl grey), maybe a little cardboard, oranges, a slight soapinessРђд It remains good but the wow factor is gone. Finish: a notch short, malty. Some grass in the aftertaste but the peaches havenРђЎt gone away. Comments: not everything is perfect but this is a very fine dram, easy to find and quite cheap at auctions. SGP:441 - 85 points.

whisky_name_raw: Tamdhu 10 yo (40%, OB, 2013)
review_text: Tamdhu 10 yo (40%, OB, 2013) I quite like this art deco bottle and label, I have to say. Nice nostalgic look. Colour: gold. Nose: very different from the older 10. This is rounder and maltier at the same time, with a little ginger from the oak and then rather coffee, honey, curious hints of tequila, fudge and raisins. Maybe still a little РђўunmingledРђЎ since this was bottled not a long time ago (new whiskies need a little rest in my opinion) but pleasure is here. A very easy nose. After ten minutes: serious, aged tequila! Mouth: starts a little heavy on the caramel and fudge, caf├Е latte, apple skins, grass and stout, but itРђЎs good. Would rather go on with white pepper, even green peppercorns, cocoa and bitter oranges. The oak remains noticeable but again, this was just bottled a few weeks ago. Finish: medium length, with more grass and herbs, much drier than expected. A little curry, pepper, black teaРђд Sweet mustard and kirsch in the aftertaste. Comments: I believe this is a 100% sherry version, but itРђЎs rather dry and leafy sherry Certainly good. SGP:361 - 83 points.

whisky_name_raw: Monte Alban (40%, OB, mezcal, +/-2013)
review_text: Monte Alban (40%, OB, mezcal, +/-2013) Con gusano indeed, this is the one that comes Рђўwith a little bottle filled with a whole mess of worms, so never again will anyone feel left out at not getting to crunch down on a preserved larvaРђЎ according to the intrepid people at Master of MaltРђЎs. Colour: white wine. Nose: much less aromas, this one is almost bland after the La Penca. There is gherkin (maybe), charcoal smoke (for sure), something like old vase water, a little incense, incense smokeРђд and thatРђЎs pretty all. Excuse me? No, I have no ideas how dead worms smell, do they smell ofРђд incense? Mouth: waaah, this is much more difficult. First, itРђЎs bizarrely oily and you cannot not imagine this is worm РђўstuffРђЎ. Then itРђЎs got quite some cardboard and plastic and then mustard. Mustard mixed with lemon juice and vinegar. RightРђд Finish: and itРђЎs long! Lemon-scented soap and litres of brine. Comments: itРђЎs not nasty stuff and maybe mescal freaks do like this one, but I think IРђЎve gone beyond my personal limits, muchacho. And no I wonРђЎt crunch the worm, GEDDIT?! SGP:362 - around 60 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glenglassaugh 33 yo 1979/2012 (44.2%, Signatory, Cask Strength, hogshead, cask #1548, 230 bottles)
review_text: Glenglassaugh 33 yo 1979/2012 (44.2%, Signatory, Cask Strength, hogshead, cask #1548, 230 bottles) Colour: straw. Nose: nice start on fresh butter and apple peelings, with mineral touches to go with all that (wet gravel). ItРђЎs very grassy in fact, with more and more cut lawn, fern, leavesРђд Also linseed oil, graphite oil, almondsРђд ItРђЎs austere whisky but I enjoy this style. Noses much younger than it actually is, obvious refill hogshead. How many refills? Mouth: starts both slightly thin (because of the strength?) and interestingly oily and lemony. ThereРђЎs a feeling ofРђд basil and oregano but also some oils and nuts. Pretty unusual, especially since it tends to become more herbal and a wee-tad earthy. Chartreuse? Finish: quite long, with more lemon and more bitter almonds as well. The aftertaste remains herbal. Comments: maybe that wasnРђЎt obvious from my notes, but I enjoyed this baby a lot. It needs time but itРђЎs extremely rewarding. SGP:461 - 90 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glenglassaugh 37 yo 1974 (48.1%, Scotch Malt Whisky Society, #21.28, 'Relaxing in a tropical garden', 188 bottles, +/-2012)
review_text: Glenglassaugh 37 yo 1974 (48.1%, Scotch Malt Whisky Society, #21.28, 'Relaxing in a tropical garden', 188 bottles, +/-2012) Colour: deep gold. Nose: a lot happening in this one! We get the same grassy and mineral notes as in the 1979 but itРђЎs all coated with fruitier and jammier touches. In other words, a fun mixture of orange liqueurs with cough syrup and olive oil. After ten minutes, we have more juicy raisins and, indeed, dates. Lovely nose, complex and movie-esque. Meaning it changes a lot, always for the better. The olive oil never stops growing and I love olive oil. Mouth: flipside, thereРђЎs a lot of oak. But a lot of oak can also mean more oils, herbs and spices and when itРђЎs all balanced, well, it works. So yeah, we have sawdust and a glue-ish side, but the way it unfolds on citrus fruits and jams, dried fruits, bananas, coconut (wee touches) and pineapples is rather entrancing. And again, it just wouldnРђЎt stop improving, even the oak gets tamed after one or two minutes, which is pretty miraculous. Finish: long, spicy and jammy. ItРђЎs only in the aftertaste that the heavy oak becomes a little embarrassing, that pat isnРђЎt too enjoyable. You have to keep sipping away s that the aftertaste never happens. Err, that would be costlyРђд Comments: I just couldnРђЎt go to 90+ because of the heavy oak, but with maybe 10 or 20% less woodiness, this would be an utter winner in my book. Hope the excellent new owners will manage to deal with this kind of situation. SGP:572 - 89 points.

whisky_name_raw: Caol Ila 30 yo 1980/2011 (46%, Mo Or Collection, bourbon hogshead, cask #2570, 300 bottles)
review_text: Caol Ila 30 yo 1980/2011 (46%, Mo Or Collection, bourbon hogshead, cask #2570, 300 bottles) Colour: pale gold. Nose: hold on, are you sure this is Caol Ila? ItРђЎs more medicinal than Laphroaig, more coastal than Ardbeg and more tarry than Port Ellen! Having said that, it all remains smooth and easy, but it does remind me more of pre-extension Coal Ila, such as the late-1960s vintages. Just ask Gordon & MacPhail. At random, I also find anchovies, bandages, olive oil, pitch, coal, seafood (crabs from the sound of Islay?) and touches of camphor. No need to say that all this is just perfect. Mouth: perfect. Perfect balance, saltiness, lemon, fish, seashells, cough lozenges, kippers, salmiak, anchovies, urchins, oysters, clamsРђд On second thought, maybe not clams. Perfect. Finish: good length, clean, complex, salty, coastal. No clams, not even in the aftertaste, which is tarrier. Comments: these 1980 Caol Ila are boringly excellent. Can we have a bad one please? SGP:456 - 91 points.

whisky_name_raw: Caol Ila 1980/2013 'The Smokery' (46%, Wemyss Malts, hogshead, 322 bottles)
review_text: Caol Ila 1980/2013 'The Smokery' (46%, Wemyss Malts, hogshead, 322 bottles) Colour: white wine. Nose: this is so different from the Mo Or! On the one hand, itРђЎs cleaner, more mineral and peatier Caol Ila. On the other hand, itРђЎs narrower and less complex. The colour is lighter but there is more oak influence, especially vanilla. More than sulphur, caramel or even free radicals ;-), excessive vanilla is the worst thing that can happen to malt whisky in my opinion. And believe me, some distillers are committing suicide these days, unknowingly. Trumpetting suicide! But rant over, that is not the case at all here, this is a beautiful old Caol Ila, just simpler and also smokier than its bro. Also wonderful notes of smoked salted almonds. Mouth: once again, this is a little simpler but on the other hand, the lemon is louder and lemon would just lift the crappiest spirit in my experience. So yeah, lemon, oysters and kippers, all that smoked to perfection. More brine after a moment, while the spirit looses a bit of steamРђд While getting earthier as wellРђд Saltier as wellРђд Finish: longer, very salty. Amazingly salty in fact, what happened? And thereРђЎs even a little chilli in the aftertaste, as well as much more smoke a d a feeling of lemonade. Comments: a fun old Caol Ila that started gentle and civilised and got wilder and wilder. The ExpendablesРђЎ favourite? Oh crapРђд SGP:356 - 89 points.

whisky_name_raw: Macallan 15 yo 1998/2013 (46%, The Coopers Choice, hogshead, cask #9452, 345 bottles)
review_text: Macallan 15 yo 1998/2013 (46%, The Coopers Choice, hogshead, cask #9452, 345 bottles) Colour: straw. Nose: lovely at first sniffs, itРђЎs a big distillate with little sherry influence, if any. Not too sure because there is a wee raisiny side, with also walnuts, so this could be a sherry hogshead as well. Other than that, a lot of Ovaltine, fudge or millionaire shortbread and then totally massive notes of milk chocolate. A creamy one such as Lindt (IРђЎm sorry, I know I shouldnРђЎt quote brand names, this is just lazy writing). Also a little menthol. ItРђЎs superb distillate and the one of the maltiest malts. Mouth: yes itРђЎs an excellent one again, very malty again, chocolaty, with some fudge and cappuccino as well as caramel and sweet liquorice. Also candy sugar and thenРђд even more chocolate. Marmalade. Finish: long, more on Seville oranges and vanilla. Oh, and chocolate. Slightly bitter and smoky aftertaste, very pleasant. Schweppes. Comments: very classy lightly РђЊ or un-sherried Macallan. Archetypical Рђўmalt whiskyРђЎ. SGP:552 - 88 points.

whisky_name_raw: Macallan 21 yo 1990/2011 (52.7%, Douglas Laing, Director's Cut, refill hogshead, DL Ref #7565, 267 bottles)
review_text: Macallan 21 yo 1990/2011 (52.7%, Douglas Laing, Director's Cut, refill hogshead, DL Ref #7565, 267 bottles) Colour: white wine. Nose: much pleasant again but itРђЎs having trouble after the 1998. This 1990 is curiously younger and harsher, with pears and apples everywhere as well as beer, leaven and dairy cream. Also limestone. With water: more rocks, limestone, whiffs of vase water and leatherette. Bizarre, bizarre, did you say bizarre? Mouth (neat): a little difficult, with a soapiness and rather heavy paraffin, then more bitter/waxy flavours and a prickliness. Gin fizz. A strange baby to say the least, very un-Macallan. With water: becomes bitter and extremely grassy. The exact opposite of the 1998. Finish: long, grassy, bitter. Ashy and sooty aftertaste, which isnРђЎt un-nice. Comments: some parts are intriguing and interesting but the whole is challenging. I know those words kill but I couldnРђЎt find better ones. Soundly beaten by the younger CooperРђЎs! SGP:371 - 76 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glenugie 1978/1994 (57.1%, Scotch Malt Whisky Society, #99, 17.5cl)
review_text: Glenugie 1978/1994 (57.1%, Scotch Malt Whisky Society, #99, 17.5cl) Not too sure whether this baby was only bottled in a small bottle or not. It only says '99', which is Glenugie's number at the SMWS as you may know. Nah, only total Glenugie freaks know thatРђд Colour: dark amber. Nose: oh hell, utter sherry! Even QuentinРђЎs movies are weak when compared with this brutal beast thatРђЎs quite hard to nose. Walnut stain? A fistful of old cigars? A whole jabugo ham? Fresh paint? All sorts of raw wild mushrooms? Oil? Chocolate sauce? Indeed, a total beast that may really need water. ItРђЎs almost straight heavily fortified palo cortado. The only little flaw is that GlenugieРђЎs magnificent distillate just wouldnРђЎt make it through the brilliant yet heavy-ish sherry. With water: the spirit doesnРђЎt seem to manage to make it any more through, but the sherryРђЎs magnificent, soРђд Mouth (neat): is this legal? Pungent, harsh, extremely leafy, rawРђд and very powerful. I donРђЎt think it works very well at this point, water is probably de rigueur. The spirit is completely dominated again. With water: ah yes, the spirit does come through despite the persisting leafy side. Mangos, papayas, guavas and suchРђд Hello, Glenugie, that was about time! Finish: long, full, rich, with more menthol and camphor. The wood was loud as well. Comments: these whiskies do not exist these days, nobody makes them like this anymore. ItРђЎs totally un-commercial, IРђЎd even say itРђЎs got a few minor flaws (the woodРђЎs becoming drying, the distilleryРђЎs character is hard to find and so onРђд) but what a ride! SGP:462 - 90 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glenugie 32 yo 1977/2010 (58.6%, Signatory Vintage, hogshead/sherry butt #1, 670 bottles)
review_text: Glenugie 32 yo 1977/2010 (58.6%, Signatory Vintage, hogshead/sherry butt #1, 670 bottles) This one was finished for 90 months in oloroso, so it's more double maturation. Colour: full amber. Nose: weРђЎre so much in line with the 1978, itРђЎs frightening. The 15+ extra-years did not change much, this is just a little leafier and less chocolaty. The almondy, putty-like side is wonderful too. With water: hell, tobacco! Leaves! Thuja wood! Pu-erh tea! Fabulous nose if youРђЎre into this styleРђд Mouth (neat): ooooohРђд the SMWS is crushed when weРђЎre talking unreduced spirit. Indeed, this babyРђЎs stronger but itРђЎs more complex, richer, more silky in a wayРђд Stunning oranges and kumquats, bergamots, oily and mentholated things (I donРђЎt know, like sauces, cordials, syrupsРђд) ItРђЎs exactly fabulous whisky and IРђЎm not even sure it needs water. Yup I know it was bottled at almost 60% vol. Yet, with water: oh no, it does not swim! The oak comes out and kills the rest, with a Рђўcocoa powder effectРђЎ thatРђЎs not too nice. Careful with water. Finish: very long, dry, mentholated. After Eights without the lethal amount of sugar. Comments: pfff, this baby is very hard to follow. Some parts were utterly brilliant while some others were kind of incoherent, for lack of a better term. And it hates water! SGP:471 - 86 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glen Scotia 1991/2013 'Salted Caramels' (46%, Wemyss Malts, barrel, 279 bottles)
review_text: Glen Scotia 1991/2013 'Salted Caramels' (46%, Wemyss Malts, barrel, 279 bottles) Picture is of last year's bottling. What, salted caramel? We come, we rush, we fly! Colour: gold. Nose: well itРђЎs more mineral than РђўcaramelyРђЎ at first nosing, and indeed quite salty (sea water). I know, salt does not smell strong. I also get rubber bands and a little hay but all that isnРђЎt very aromatic. An austere nose, but at least itРђЎs not feinty (some GS could be feinty in my book) and I have to say I like these whiffs of brine and seawater that never stop becoming louder. LetРђЎs hope the caramel will be found on the palateРђд Mouth: well IРђЎm no salted caramel expert, but I do not think this tastes like salted caramels. Or do our friend the Scots make a specific one? Instead, we have some peat, some black olives, some soft chilli, a saltiness indeed and then rather massive notes of salmiak РђЊ or salted liquorice. Do the Scots have caramel that tastes like liquorice? Finish: long, salty, liquoricy. There is, indeed, some toffee in the aftertaste. Comments: this is much to my liking, the spirit has a lot to tell us. I was joking about the caramel, there is some caramel but thereРђЎs even more liquorice. There. SGP:462 - 87 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glen Scotia 20 yo 1992/2012 (50.6%, The Whisky Cask, bourbon hogshead)
review_text: Glen Scotia 20 yo 1992/2012 (50.6%, The Whisky Cask, bourbon hogshead) Colour: gold. Nose: ho-ho-ho, this is a huge jar full of linseed oil, motor oil, almonds, paraffin and, above all, something thatРђЎs not very common in whisky, sesame oil. Or even smoked sesame oil. Very curious nowРђд With water: it changes a lot, becoming very ashy and sooty. Very dry. Touches of moss and dead leaves. Very humussy (is that a proper word, S.?) Mouth (neat): g.e.n.t.i.a.n.!!! Earthy, peppery, rooty and slightly rubbery gentian! LetРђЎs hold our horses, this is no easy whisky actually, some parts are lovely and oh-so-smoky-old-Highlands, but this harsh bitterness can be a little off-putting. With water: the juryРђЎs still out, some parts are charmingly old-skool (the smokiness) but others are frankly difficult. Papery salt or salty paper? Finish: quite long, very briny now. Maybe there are even one or two slices ofРђд gherkins? Comments: this is pretty extreme stuff, for aficionados only. Do not pour this to the housekeeper, or your life will change forever. And not for the betterРђд But I love the gentiany side! SGP:362 - 82 points.

whisky_name_raw: Talisker 'Dark Storm' (45.8%, OB, travel retail exclusive, 2013)
review_text: Talisker 'Dark Storm' (45.8%, OB, travel retail exclusive, 2013) This one was matured in charred casks, which has been 'ensuring an inimitable spiciness and distinctive smoky character' according to a high-ranked exec. What, charred oak makes whisky smokier? Let's see... Colour: golden orange. Nose: raw, peaty, almost harsh, pungent, spirity, a little mentholated... Well, its maybe not a whisky for nosing, I wouldn't say it's overly aromatic. Maybe also a little more medicinal than others? Mouth: ah yes, now we're talking. It's probably very young but peat and vanilla combine well here and it's got good oomph and zing. At first it's a rather rounder and creamier expression of young Talisker but it kept the spicy and peaty signature (it's actually very peaty). Don't look for a lot of complexity but balance and character are there in abundance. Finish: good length, with a vanilla that's a little more in the front. A feeling of salted caramel. Comments: I think I like this new one almost as much as I liked Storm. Ah, and no need to spend a lot of time just nosing it! Definitely modern and both spirit and wood-driven. SGP:457 - 86 points.

whisky_name_raw: Talisker 25 yo (45.8%, OB, refill casks, 5,946 bottles, 2011)
review_text: Talisker 25 yo (45.8%, OB, refill casks, 5,946 bottles, 2011) I think this was the first 25yo bottled at Talisker's usual lower strength instead of the previous 'high strength' levels. The last earlier version that we've tried was the 2008 at 54.2%, brilliant stuff (WF 91). Colour: full gold. Nose: a completely different planet after the Dark Storm, this is smoother of course, but also more complex, with some lilac, tangerines, roses, almonds, this very 'tropical' smokiness that only old peaters can display, beauty cream, brine, oysters... It's a lovely nose, delicate and subtle. Mouth: once again, a slightly smoother version of an old Talisker but power is there. It's no straight brute as older versions could be (remember the 62% vol? Or was that the 20yo?) but it's not lacking oomph. Smoked overripe apples and lemon curd plus brine and kippers. There. Finish: long, maybe a notch woodier, with this feeling of strong green tea but also a perfect brine. Little pepper this time. Comments: I know some whisky lovers and Talisker aficionados were shocked by the drop in strength and in theory, I should be shocked too, but let's be honest, this remains a mightily powerful dram. SGP:457 - 90 points.

whisky_name_raw: Miltonduff 30 yo 1980/2010 (46%, Mo Or Collection, bourbon hogshead, cask #12431, 321 bottles)
review_text: Miltonduff 30 yo 1980/2010 (46%, Mo Or Collection, bourbon hogshead, cask #12431, 321 bottles) Colour: gold. Nose: I think 30 years old is the perfect age for malt whisky matured in not-too-lazy-not-too-active oak, and this is another fine example. I think Samaroli had similar casks, this is very complex, slightly shy and very elegant whisky. So we have dozens of herbal teas, rather on the anise-y side (caraway, aniseed, fennel and such), also lime and orange blossom, then more almond oil, olive oil and argan oil (I swear to sweet Vishnu). I also enjoy this growing leafy/grassy side, making it resemble an old Habanero. Old Lusitania, anyone? What a nose! Mouth: superb! Almost a revelation, with a perfect combination of spicy, herbal and fruity notes. Ultra-complex whisky, so totally un-modern, without what may kill malt whisky in the coming decades: excessive vanilla. My opinionРђд Finish: long, perfect, with just the right amount of everything. Sucking a cigar that was dipped into honey (ma que horror!) Only the aftertaste is a notch too dry and bitter. Comments: itРђЎs confirmed, there are/were absolute gems in this Mo Or series! Sheer class at a perfect drinking strength. SGP:462 - 91 points.

whisky_name_raw: Miltonduff-Glenlivet 22 yo 1990/2012 (56.7%, Cadenhead, wine cask, Claret)
review_text: Miltonduff-Glenlivet 22 yo 1990/2012 (56.7%, Cadenhead, wine cask, Claret) Claret is the name that the Britons used to use for Bordeaux, so I guess this is from a Bordeaux cask. Colour: gold. Nose: what is this? DonРђЎt get me wrong, I enjoy this quite a lot, but itРђЎs unusual. Campari-Orange? Bellini cocktail? Blood oranges? Pink grapefruits? Indeed weРђЎre not quite in classic malt territories but I have to say itРђЎs all very much to my liking so far (says the guy who looks for his gun as soon as he sees red wine in whisky). Bah, the colour wasnРђЎt pink anyway ;-)Рђд With water: more of the same, which is great news. Maybe did the Campari-ish side get even louder? Mouth (neat): I love to be proven wrong as far as whiskyРђЎs concerned, and indeed this is a wine cask that worked perfectly well. Did they use 1945 Lafite? 1986 Mouton? Perfect Seville oranges blended with ginger and red berries. Indeed, works extremely well. With water: oh! Water (my usual Vittel) made it extremely thick and syrupy, in a good way. Is that the probable French oak? Gingered maple syrup plus blood oranges and pepper. Finish: very long, spicier but still balanced. Comments: I hereby solemnly declare that this baby was my favourite red-wine-Scotch ever, whether full-matured or just aromatised/finished. Now, am I really sound in body and mind? SGP:562 - 90 points.

whisky_name_raw: Clynelish 24 yo 1988/2013 (48.8%, Alexander, SwissLink 4, refill bourbon hogshead, cask #4550)
review_text: Clynelish 24 yo 1988/2013 (48.8%, Alexander, SwissLink 4, refill bourbon hogshead, cask #4550) After the 1970s, the 1980s are becoming rare. IРђЎve just checked that this is the first time I try a 1988! Colour: white wine. Nose: sometimes Clynelish can be a little closed at first, and thatРђЎs what happens here. ThatРђЎs why some malt lovers have sometimes problems with Clynelish, they donРђЎt give them enough time in my opinion. Anyway, this baby starts mineral and grassy, with only touches of earth/roots and a little pear in the background; LetРђЎs let it breathe a bitРђд Рђд. Рђд. (after fifteen minutes) right, there is a little more wax and bergamot, as well as some fresh mint and eucalyptus leaves, but it remains kind of shy and restrained. Mouth: ah yes, now weРђЎre talking. Grapefruits and orange zests, light kumquats (well, kumquats), bitter pears, cider applesРђд But almost no wax. Finish: good length, even more on apples. Comments: I enjoyed this one but maybe itРђЎs a little narrow. SGP:441 - 84 points.

whisky_name_raw: Clynelish 16 yo 1996/2013 (53.3%, Whisky-F├цssle, sherry cask)
review_text: Clynelish 16 yo 1996/2013 (53.3%, Whisky-F├цssle, sherry cask) In theory, we should have had this one before the Swiss, but as the strength is higher, I thought weРђЎd have it as #2. LetРђЎs see if that was a mistakeРђд Colour: dark gold. Nose: what is this? One of the most un-Clynelish Clynelishes I could nose, with a dry sherry thatРђЎs rather dominating. Then earth, parsley, soy sauce, struck matches, metal (mumРђЎs old aluminium pans) and gentian eau-de-vie. Little Clynelishness, even after ten minutes or more. Maybe water is the keyРђд With water: becomes gamy, with even more matches. Or brown coal? Cigars? Tarmac? Soy sauce and chicken soup? Mouth (neat): intriguing, the combination of a leafy sherry with the big spirit thatРђЎs Clynelish is almost creating a third dimension, between oxtail soup and walnut wine. Some sweet mustard too, or some kind of yet-unknown Outer-Mongolian sauce to go with yak meat? Whaf! Pepper. With water: ah, becomes disappointingly normal now. IРђЎm joking, this is much better. ItРђЎs even a little briny. Manzanilla (IРђЎm thinking of LustauРђЎs Papirusa). Finish: long, salty, dry, even more РђўmanzanillaРђЎ. Comments: maybe one for malt lovers whoРђЎve already got two or four Clynelishes in their stash, as this oneРђЎs quite atypical. But it tells you many storiesРђд SGP:352 - 87 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glenrothes 1995/2011 (43%, OB)
review_text: Glenrothes 1995/2011 (43%, OB) There was a 1995/2010 that was very pleasant (WF 83). Colour: pale gold. Nose: oh yeah, honey and roasted nuts all over the place, as well as a lot of malt, rather Ovaltine-style. Add to that notes of tarte tatin, caramel and warm praline and youРђЎll get a rather accurate picture. Very typical middle-aged Glenrothes. Also touches of walnuts and sweet wine coming through later. Dried figs. Mouth: a little narrower but pleasantly malty and fudge-y, with also some chocolate, raisins, honey sauce and orange cake. ItРђЎs a smooth and easy tipple, with good body but of course itРђЎs no big beast. Nice touches of grass and green tea as well. Finish: of medium length, honeyed, with roasted peanuts and maybe pecans. Nope, no cashews this time РђЊ but maybe Macadamia nuts? Orange marmalade in the aftertaste. Comments: good and easy, very РђўmediumРђЎ, whatever that means. The definition of malt whisky, in a way. I like it. SGP:441 - 84 points.

whisky_name_raw: Glenrothes 17 yo 1995/2013 (46%, Signatory Vintage, 1st fill sherry butt, cask #6972, 761 bottles)
review_text: Glenrothes 17 yo 1995/2013 (46%, Signatory Vintage, 1st fill sherry butt, cask #6972, 761 bottles) A pretty great sister cask was bottled for Waldhaus am See in Switzerland in 2012. Colour: amber. Nose: this is funny, this is a single cask by a well-reputed indie and the whiskyРђЎs the same as the official. That very rarely happens in my experience. After a few minutes, maybe this oneРђЎs got added touches of shoe polish and a little gunpowder, as well as a little cough mixtureРђд Very nice nose, quite complex and somewhat old-style. Mouth: once again, itРђЎs the OB with a little more oomph, gunpowder, wax and menthol. And liquorice. If the OB was going to ten, this one goes to eleven, Nigel. Finish: good, long, malty, honeyed, roasted and cake-y. A pleasant earthiness in the aftertaste. Sweet malt. Comments: all good. These bottlings often go unnoticed, especially since the excellent bottlers are much quieter than others (who have more friends on facebook than bottles ;-)). A bottling thatРђЎs much to my liking again. SGP:551 - 86 points.
